data.title: Palantir: Nothing Accelerates Forever (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Let me begin by saying that I was wrong.

My previous two "Sell" articles on Palantir (NASDAQ:PLTR) were far from accurate (you can find the first article here and the second article here). Since those articles, Palantir stock went up another 75% and 52%, respectively. If you followed my recommendation, you would've missed out on a lot of potential gains.

Don't get me wrong. I think Palantir stock is still overvalued, so I'm not recommending a Buy at these levels. On the other hand, I wouldn't completely sell out of my position either, let alone short it.

Perhaps, the best course of action might just be holding it for the long run, especially considering Palantir's growing importance in the emerging AI economy.

Fundamentals: There's No Denying It

Palantir is a software company that helps enterprises integrate their data, decisions, and operations at scale. The company offers four main platforms, namely: Gotham, Foundry, Apollo, and AIP. These platforms are incredibly versatile, allowing organizations across the public, private, and non-profit sectors to leverage Palantir's game-changing software to maximize efficiency and productivity.

Below are some of its use cases:

Palantir's defining moment came when it launched AIP, its differentiated artificial intelligence platform, which, one could argue, has single-handedly revived the company from the ashes. In essence, AIP helps enterprises bridge the gap between AI and their day-to-day operations. While other companies race to develop the best large language model, Palantir stands alone in building the ontology to help enterprises apply AI to their operations.

This is what sets Palantir apart, and it is clearly reflected in its financials.

Q1 Revenue came in at $884M, up 39% YoY, beating estimates by $22M and guidance by $24M. As you can see, growth has accelerated for the seventh straight quarter, signifying tremendous business momentum.

Looking at the different Revenue segments, Palantir's US Commercial segment continued its blistering growth, up 71% YoY, while its Government segment, as a whole, delivered outstanding results, up 45% YoY across the globe. Significant demand for Palantir's Warp Speed (manufacturing OS) and Maven Smart System (AI-enabled military software) was a key driver of growth.

International Commercial, on the other hand, was down 5% YoY, due to continued headwinds in Europe. While the slowdown in this segment may be concerning, growing the International Commercial segment is not management's current priority — they are more interested in accelerating the US business. Nevertheless, there are reasons to be optimistic about the International Commercial segment, as the performance seen in the US could very well be replicated on international grounds.

Despite the decline in International Commercial Revenue, Palantir's International Commercial Customers grew 15% YoY to 190. US Commercial Customers grew 65% YoY to 432. Total Commercial Customers grew 46% YoY to 622.

Government Customers grew 16% YoY to 147. On aggregate, Total Customers grew 39% YoY to 769.

The number of customers is a leading indicator of future Revenue growth, so such strong customer growth reflects Palantir's increasing Revenue potential. It is also worth mentioning that Revenue Per Top 20 Customers grew 26% YoY to a record $70M, implying Palantir's expanding use case and customers' commitment to using Palantir's software.

Importantly, Palantir maintains a robust deal pipeline, with 139 deals of at least $1M closed in Q1. Moreover, Palantir booked its highest quarter of US Commercial TCV of $810M, up a staggering 183% YoY.

As a result of strong Q1 growth, management raised their full-year 2025 Revenue guidance by $147M to $3.890B to $3.902B, representing a 36% annual growth rate. By the same token, management also raised their 2025 US Commercial Revenue growth guidance by 14pp, to at least 68% annual growth. This is a testament to Palantir's unique dominance in AI enterprise software. Moreover, this guidance could still be sandbagged, as Palantir has repeatedly destroyed its guidance over the past couple of years.

This should translate to massive earnings growth in the coming quarters.

In Q1, Non-GAAP EPS was $0.13, in line with estimates.

Palantir generated $391M of Adjusted Operating Income at a 44% Margin, which expanded 8pp YoY. Management expects earnings to continue its upward trend, guiding for full-year 2025 Adjusted Operating Income of $1.711B to $1.723B. This guidance was raised by $158M at the midpoint, implying strong operating leverage.

Free Cash Flow for the quarter was $304M at a 34% FCF Margin, which expanded 14pp YoY. In the trailing twelve months, FCF was $1.3B at a 42% Margin, its highest figure ever, both on an absolute and marginal basis. In addition, management also raised their FCF guidance by $0.1B, to $1.6B to $1.8B.

As you can see, Palantir is delivering exceptional results. Growth and profitability are accelerating, resulting in a Rule of 40 score of 83%, its highest quarterly figure to date. Management also issued new guidance of a Rule of 40 score of 80% for the full year. In other words, they are expecting robust growth and margins moving forward.

All things considered, this is execution on an elite level — there's no denying it.

Valuation: Comparing Two Other Stocks With 100x Sales Multiples

While Palantir's fundamentals keep stacking higher and higher, its valuation may have gotten way ahead of itself. For context, Palantir is currently trading at a price-to-sales Ratio of 102x, which is extremely expensive.

The only other software companies that I'm aware of that have a PS Ratio of over 100x were Cloudflare (NET) and Snowflake (SNOW) in 2021. At that time, Palantir was trading at a PS Ratio of about 40x. In the following few years, all three stocks crashed more than 70%, with their valuation multiples compressing below 20x.

Today, Cloudflare and Snowflake trade at a PS Ratio of 35x and 18x, respectively. Palantir, on the other hand, is behaving differently, with multiples expanding to more than 100x as of today.

So why has Palantir's valuation multiple expanded while its peers remain depressed?

The answer lies with fundamental acceleration.

As you can see below, the growth rates of each of the three companies decelerated from 2021 to 2023, which partly explains the stocks' eventual decline.

However, Palantir's growth started to accelerate in mid-2023, while Cloudflare and Snowflake continued to decelerate. This explains why Palantir's valuation multiple expanded while its peers' multiples remained well below their peak levels.

It's also worth pointing out that Palantir has already turned GAAP profitable, whereas Cloudflare and Snowflake have not, so Palantir's profitability also played a major role in its premium valuation.

Furthermore, the sentiment around AI is boosting Palantir's share price, while Cloudflare and Snowflake did not have a strong enough narrative to sustain their valuations.

In short, the acceleration of fundamentals and the AI narrative drove Palantir's astronomical rise, whereas in the case of Cloudflare and Snowflake, unprofitability, decelerating growth, and the lack of a solid narrative pressured their valuations.

That being said, as long as Palantir's fundamentals continue to accelerate, I believe the stock will continue to rise. However, if growth slows down, I think things could turn ugly really fast. For now, Palantir's updated guidance points to further growth acceleration for another quarter or two, which could mean more upside in the near term.

By traditional valuation metrics and models, Palantir is extremely overvalued — there's no way around it. Even my Bull-case scenario, which assumes a 30% CAGR over the next 10 years and a long-term FCF Margin of 50% (yes, these are very aggressive assumptions), returned a price target of “only” $82. This represents a downside of about 36%.

However, Palantir's growth is still accelerating, and its narrative as the most important AI company in the West still rings true. This is why, despite its 100x PS Ratio, I have a Hold rating on Palantir stock.

Risks

As I said, if growth slows down, Palantir's premium valuation may be at risk. At 100x its Revenue, there's basically zero room for error.

So far, Palantir's execution has been perfect, thus the share price explosion, and perhaps, shareholders may get to enjoy a few more quarters of acceleration.

Just be wary that nothing accelerates forever.

Thesis

There's no doubt that Palantir has exceptional fundamentals with a compelling growth story fuelled by AI. However, it seems Palantir's valuation indicates that at least 5 years' worth of growth has already been priced in, so the risk-to-reward may not be attractive at current prices.

It's hard to decide whether to buy or sell. On one hand, fundamentals are set to accelerate further in the next few quarters, which may point to more upside in the near term. On the other hand, nothing accelerates forever, and a slowdown in the business may spell trouble for a company trading at 100x its Revenue.

For these reasons, I think it's best to just hold the stock while keeping position sizes at a manageable level.

data.title: XRT: Resilient Retail Amid The Tariff War
data.description:
All eyes are on the consumer. Last Friday’s May jobs report was better than expected on the headline payrolls number, but some of the report’s internals were troubling, including a sharp drop in the household survey’s employment, while average hourly earnings rose, suggesting that there could still be some lingering inflationary pressure. Moreover, sharp downward revisions to the March and April reports proved that labor-market momentum wasn’t all it was cracked up to be heading into May.

Still, stocks rallied, and gains were healthy in the S&P Retail ETF (NYSEARCA:XRT). I had a buy rating on the consumer fund back in August 2024, and I reiterate a buy rating today. Shares are up just 3% since my previous assessment, underperforming the S&P 500 by about four percentage points.

Amid solid weekly retail sales figures via the Johnson Redbook Index and all-time highs in bellwether equities like Visa (V) and Mastercard (MA), consumers keep on spending. So long as they have jobs and are earning above-inflation wages, then it should be smooth sailing for the consumption portion of GDP.

Let’s dive back into XRT to see where its valuation, portfolio, and technicals stand as we head into the summer vacation season.

Johnson Redbook Weekly Retail Sales Still Firm, Near +5% YoY

Visa and Mastercard Shares Hitting All-Time Highs

According to the issuer, XRT seeks to provide exposure to the retail segment of the S&P Total Market Index, which comprises the following sub-industries: Apparel Retail, Automotive Retail, Computer & Electronic Retail, Department Stores, Drug Retail, Food Retailers, Broadline Retail, Consumer Staples Merchandise Retail, and Specialty Stores. The ETF features a modified equal-weighted approach, which provides the potential for unconcentrated industry exposure across large, mid, and small-cap stocks.

XRT is a small ETF with just $400 million in assets under management as of June 5, 2025—that's down from $425 million at the time of my Q3 2024 analysis. Its annual expense ratio is low to moderate at 35 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is 1.60%, which is about 30 basis points above that of the S&P 500.

Share-price momentum has improved markedly since just a few months ago, earning the product a solid B ETF Grade in that category from Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. The fund’s risk level remains high, however, and it can swing around greatly during retail earnings season—the report this coming week to watch will be RH (RH), among the most in-focus companies amid the ongoing tariff war.

Finally, XRT is highly liquid, given an average daily volume of more than 7 million shares and a 30-day median bid/ask spread of just a single basis point, per SSGA Funds.

For an update on the portfolio, XRT has high cyclical exposure not only from holding a Consumer Discretionary-centric allocation but also from its high weight to domestic small and mid-cap equities. As an improved macro situation unfolds, XRT should outperform. Of course, if Elon Musk is right, and we see a second-half tariff-induced recession, the retail ETF would likely underperform.

But with a low 15.3x price-to-earnings ratio, there isn’t a ton of optimism priced in. We also see measured earnings growth forecasts—recall that many mid-sized retailers either withdrew or brought down their full-year 2025 profit guidance over the past few weeks. Firms that were upbeat and raised their financial outlooks were generally rewarded by Wall Street. Of course, said companies now have a higher bar to clear.

XRT: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

As mentioned previously, XRT is the majority comprised of Consumer Discretionary stocks. Being a modified equal-weighted product, the average market cap size is also quite small. The top 10 holdings will often be its biggest winners over the last handful of weeks, and that’s what we see today.

Still, no single name will have an outsized impact on the fund. I’d also call out that XRT has paid out higher dividends in the last few years compared with previous cycles.

XRT: Holdings & Dividend Information

From a seasonal perspective, June and July are the ETF’s best back-to-back months, so we are in the heart of strong calendar trends. With rising price action, I expect further gains as we venture into the third quarter.

XRT: Bullish June-July Trends

The Technical Take

With a decent valuation and improved momentum, XRT’s technicals are mixed, but lean bullish. Notice in the chart below that shares have rebounded sharply off the April post-Liberation Day lows. XRT is actually beating the S&P 500 ETF (SPY) by five percentage points since April 8. Along with relative strength, there’s absolute momentum, as seen by the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph.

Unfortunately, it’s not all roses with XRT. Take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average—it's merely flat in its slope, suggesting an ongoing battle between the bulls and bears. Moreover, there’s a high amount of volume by price from the current level up to $80. If the bulls can bring the fund through resistance at the late 2024 highs, then a test of the 2021 all-time high could be in the works.

On the downside, a gap lingers near $71—about where the 50dma comes into view.

XRT: Strong RSI Momentum, 2024 Highs In Sight

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on XRT. The retail ETF has posted notable alpha to the S&P 500 in the past two months, and with a bullish seasonal period underway, I expect further gains on the mid-teens P/E fund.

data.title: UYLD: Attractive Yield Enhancer
data.description:
Thesis

The Angel Oak Ultrashort Income ETF (NASDAQ:UYLD) is a fixed-income exchange-traded fund from the Angel Oak fund family. We have covered other names from Angel Oak before, or their baby bonds.

UYLD is a yield enhancer, with the name focused on taking reduced duration and credit risk to provide for a higher yield than Fed Funds:

The Angel Oak UltraShort Income ETF seeks to provide current income while seeking to minimize price volatility and maintain liquidity. The primary focus of Fund assets will be within the best relative value opportunities of structured credit, including RMBS, CMBS, ABS, and CLOs. The Fund may also include allocations to government bonds and agency-backed securities, which include U.S. Treasury securities, agency RMBS, and agency CMBS. The Fund’s average rate duration will be maintained below 1.0.

In today's article, we are going to have a closer look at UYLD and its structure and articulate why we find the name appealing.

Fund build

True to its objective statement, the name holds mostly investment-grade assets, which make up 96.3% of the fund. It's split by asset class as follows:

ABS securities are 31% of the holdings, followed by corporates at 17.7% and CLOs at 17%. Unlike other names in the space that use short-dated corporate bonds, UYLD tends to focus on securitized products via its build.

The ratings breakdown is as follows:

  • AAA: 38.8%
  • AA: 10.5%
  • A: 21%
  • BBB: 26%
  • Unrated & Below IG: residual

As expected, the name has a high concentration in AAA and AA tranches, the most creditworthy from a corporate or ABS structure. An investor, however, needs to understand that in the securitized space, there is no homogeneity like in the corporate space; thus, spreads are a bit wider. Also, depending on underlying risk factors and performance, pre-payments can speed up and durations can accelerate.

Let us now look at the performance

It is interesting to see how UYLD did during the recent April 2025 market risk-off event:

We are looking at a cohort of peers that includes the PIMCO Enhanced Short Maturity ETF (MINT), the Janus Henderson AAA CLO (JAAA), the SPDR Bloomberg 1-3 Month T-Bill ETF (BIL), and the VanEck CLO ETF (CLOI).

As we can observe from the above graph, JAAA and CLOI had the biggest drawdowns, while UYLD performed in line with MINT and BIL. This contrast is notable since UYLD contains mostly securitized assets; thus, the initial inclination is to put it in the same bucket as JAAA or CLOI. Thus, UYLD outperformed its asset class.

Longer term, the name has also proven itself via a high total return:

The riskier names like CLOI and JAAA have provided the largest total returns in the past three years, but UYLD produced better figures than MINT and BIL.

Yields and risk metrics

The fund targets the front end of the yield curve and contains many floating rate assets, thus its yield will always depend on Fed Funds. Its current 30-day SEC yield is 5.2%:

The ETF therefore provides for a rough 100 bps enhancement over Fed Funds, but will see its overall yield shrink if and when the Fed cuts. However, kindly keep in mind that all the competing cohorts are built the same way, so comparisons matter here.

In the table above, one should look at the 'Subsidized' yield, which simply means the name is giving some expense ratio relief for the time being:

The expense ratio will stay at 0.29% throughout the entire year in 2025 and switch to a larger figure in June 2026. Please note that the fund manager can keep subsidizing the expense ratio to a lower figure if it chooses to do so.

From a risk perspective, we have already seen how well the name did in April 2025 when its drawdown was very shallow. If we look at the Seeking Alpha platform risk analytics, we also get small figures:

The fund sports a low 0.48% standard deviation and a 0.78% annualized volatility. Those are extremely low figures for a securitized products fund that is not all AAA. Furthermore, these are exactly the types of numbers you would want to see for a cash parking vehicle.

Risk factors

The fund's effective duration is 0.7 years, thus rising interest rates do not affect this name. As we saw in April 2025, a risk-off event does not affect the fund either. The only potential risk factor is a very deep recession that would temporarily move spreads higher here. Due to its build, the name has a very, very low probability of default for its assets; thus, a recession can only cause a temporary drawdown in our view.

Furthermore, a bad recession can also cause liquidity issues for securitized products, thus moving prices lower on lack of bids.

Conclusion

UYLD is a short-dated fixed-income fund. The name counts as a yield enhancer, providing for a rough 1% spread over Fed Funds. The ETF is composed mostly of securitized products, including ABS, CLOs, and Agency MBS. The fund did very well during the April 2025 market sell-off, recording a sub -1% drawdown, much better than JAAA as an example. The fund comes with very low volatility and standard deviation figures and a 5.29% SEC yield. We like this name and own it, and consider it a reliable, robust cash parking vehicle.

data.title: CAVA Group: A True Outlier In The Restaurant Space
data.description:
Though the stock market has returned to rallying and is now in positive territory for the year, investors have to be careful not to mistake market sentiment for economic sentiment: because despite a jump in stocks, many companies reported lackluster earnings in Q1, especially those with exposure to slower consumer spending.

CAVA Group (CAVA), the Mediterranean restaurant chain, has certainly not been exempt from this overarching pullback. And yet, the company has been a true outlier in still holding on to double-digit same store sales growth, while its aggressive pace of store expansion is ensuring that the company achieves a land grab in market share while the sector is weak. Understandably, investors are nervous about valuation and that’s feeding the stock’s ~30% decline since the start of the year: yet I also think this stock has ample room for a rebound.

I last wrote a buy opinion on CAVA in February, when it was trading in the mid-$90s and before the stock market had crashed following Trump’s tariffs announcement. While it’s true that CAVA’s growth has slowed since then, it has held up much better than many key peers in a difficult macro - prompting me to reiterate my buy rating on this name.

To me, these are the core reasons to be bullish on CAVA:

  • CAVA dominates in a space with no national brand recognition. There are countless burger chains, chicken chains, burrito/Mexican chains, pizza chains, etcetera. But the rising Mediterranean category only has a truly national presence in CAVA.
  • Secular health tailwinds and broad menu options. CAVA benefits from marketing the healthy aspects of Mediterranean dining at the same time that health movements are taking off. We note as well that the breadth of the Mediterranean menu gives CAVA’s customers more options, which may increase frequency of dining.
  • Ample white space to expand in the U.S. CAVA has a bigger presence on the East Coast, and it has also moved into the Midwest. But the company has yet to establish a major presence in major areas, including California and Texas.

The biggest risk and drawback to CAVA, of course, is its valuation: despite a sharp YTD fall, CAVA has managed to maintain a substantial premium: both against the broader market and against specific restaurant peers.

At current share prices near $79, CAVA trades at a $9.07 billion market cap. After we net off the $369.3 million of cash on CAVA's latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $8.70 billion.

Meanwhile, for FY25, CAVA has updated its outlook to indicate that it will open 64-68 net-new stores, up by 4 stores at the midpoint. It's also expecting slightly more expensive pre-opening costs of $14.5-$15.5 million on average per unit (the company's current balance sheet cash can finance ~25 new openings at this rate, not including ongoing cash flow or possible debt instruments). The good news is that while the company has held on to its expectations for same-restaurant sales growth and restaurant margins, it expects these new stores to immediately contribute to the bottom line: the company slightly increased its full-year adjusted EBITDA expectations to $152-$159 million.

CAVA's valuation multiple sits at 55.5x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA. As an investor that is typically value-oriented, this premium valuation gives me pause - CAVA's multiples sit well above other key peers, such as salad upstart Sweetgreen (SG) and longtime fast-casual category leader Chipotle.

But this is where we need to consider the rarity of CAVA's growth. CAVA alone is managing double-digit same-restaurant sales in this environment: Chipotle and Sweetgreen's comps have turned slightly negative, while Shake Shack is only in the low single digits. CAVA is picking up market share in this tough environment, which buoys its plans to expand its footprint nationwide. In other words, this is still a restaurant chain in its early innings, and so near-term valuation multiples are a less reliable indicator of its future potential.

Despite this premium, I'm content to use the dip as a buying opportunity in CAVA. Stay long here.

Q1 Recap

Let's now go through CAVA's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

CAVA's overall revenue grew 28% y/y to $331.8 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $237.6 million (+26% y/y) by a two-point margin. Again here, CAVA fed its growth both via new store expansion as well as same-store sales growth. The company opened 15 net-new restaurants in the quarter (just under a quarter of its planned 64-68 openings this year), bringing its quarter-end restaurant count to 382, up 18% y/y.

Where I think CAVA shines brighter than its competition, however, is in its same-restaurant sales growth, which grew 10.8% y/y. Yes, it's true that this is the slowest pace in four quarters, though the slowdown was largely to be expected after very strong performance in the back half of FY24 (which will turn into very difficult comps in the back half of FY25).

But at the same time, CAVA is holding up exceptionally better than its peers. The chart below showcases Chipotle's comparable sales trends, which decelerated 6 points to a -0.4% decline in the same quarter:

Similarly, Sweetgreen - which for the past year has rivaled CAVA for the crown of the fastest-growing fast-casual chain - has pulled back sharply to a -3.1% decline, from mid-single digit growth in the previous few quarters.

Only CAVA has been able to maintain its double-digit same-restaurant ale growth (at least for now before tougher comps kick in). A number of factors have contributed to that success. The company continues to capitalize on seasonal menu items and new launches, including a highly popular new pita chip menu option. The company has also relaunched its loyalty program to encourage visit frequency, and is up to 8 million members. Management is also rolling out a new kitchen operating system that it says has greatly improved order throughput. Speaking to this upgrade on the Q&A portion of the Q1 earnings call, CEO Brett Schulman noted as follows:

As it relates to the KDS, we don't give specific throughput or productivity numbers, but what we can say is that the new kitchen display screen systems allow for much richer, deeper order management capabilities, including increased productivity. So, we have throttling capabilities on the back end that allow restaurants to manage how many orders they receive every 15 minutes. And these screens allow them the capacity and ability to open up those throttles to a greater degree, and most importantly, deliver improved order accuracy and guest experience scores is what we're seeing in the test restaurants. So, we've expanded the rollout from 25 to 42, and we're accelerating that over the next few weeks, working towards our goal of 250 restaurants by the end of the year."

We do note that CAVA has hit near-term snags on restaurant-level operating margins (profit margins excluding corporate overhead).

As shown in the chart above, food costs have inflated by 110 bps as a percentage of revenue, primarily due to inflation in the price of steak. The company managed to offset this headwind with efficiencies on labor and smaller rent/occupancy expenses as a percentage of revenue (as high comp sales growth and average unit revenue generates economies of scale on individual restaurant locations), allowing restaurant-level margins to pull back only -10bps y/y to 25.1%. The good news is that the company expects the cost headwinds from steak to roll off by June.

CAVA also still managed to eke out gains on adjusted EBITDA margins, with adjusted EBITDA of $331.8 million growing 28% y/y and hitting a 13.5% margin that improved 60bps y/y.

Key Takeaways

While it's true that CAVA sustained a largely expected deceleration in both overall revenue and same-restaurant sales growth in Q1, to me, the Q1 earnings season demonstrated why CAVA deserves a wide premium against its peers in the fast-casual industry, as it was the only company that has managed to sustain double-digit comps growth while key peers slipped to a y/y decline.

Despite a rich valuation and a tough macro environment, I remain confident in CAVA's ability to keep expanding its footprint nationally while building up its margin profile and gradually growing into its valuation. Stay long here and hold out for a rebound after this year's sharp dip.

data.title: UTG: Balanced Risk-Reward At Current Prices
data.description:
It's been a while since I wrote about the Reaves Utility Trust (UTG). In my previous article, I downgraded the UTG fund due to a perceived rise in regulatory risks. In hindsight, it was a painful-missed opportunity, as the UTG fund has performed handsomely since my downgrade (Figure 1).

While I may not always be correct, readers can rest assured that I will be honest and own up to my mistakes. Let's take a look at what I missed in my previous analysis and whether the UTG fund is a good buy in the current environment.

In hindsight, I did not anticipate the surge in power demand growth forecasts, as analysts plug AI assumptions into their models. AI models like ChatGPT are power hogs (~10x more computationally intensive than Google search), and utility companies are rushing to bring on new capacity to meet the anticipated demand.

This has transformed the once sleepy utility sector into a quasi-AI-growth story and has delivered handsome returns. However, adding AI-growth also adds AI-volatility, as the utility sector has been trading in lockstep with the Magnificent 7.

Overall, increased risks for the UTG fund are balanced by relatively attractive valuations. I am maintaining my hold recommendation for now.

Brief Fund Overview

The Reaves Utility Income Fund primarily invests in equities and bonds of utility companies and has a long history of paying consistent high distributions (Figure 2).

The UTG fund is a favorite among income investors, with over $2.8 billion in net assets. Currently, UTG's $0.19 / month distribution gives the UTG fund an attractive 6.6% forward distribution yield (Figure 3).

The UTG fund charges investors a relatively expensive 2.23% expense ratio.

Significant Changes To The Portfolio

While stability is the name of the game for UTG's distribution, the fund's portfolio has undergone significant changes since my last article. Compared to the portfolio as of December 31, 2023, there are only 3 companies currently in UTG's top-10 holdings that were there previously (Figure 4).

UTG has notably reduced its positions in Canadian telecom companies BCE and Telus (in fact, BCE is no longer in UTG's portfolio), while adding nuclear utilities and data center stocks like Constellation Energy (CEG) and Equinox (EQX), which are thought to benefit from the artificial intelligence boom.

AI Fever Seeps Into Utilities

In hindsight, my cautious view of the utility sector back in early 2024 failed to take into account the surge in energy demand from the rapid deployment of artificial intelligence into everyday products and services. According to a Goldman Sachs report, on average, a ChatGPT query uses 10x the power of a Google search query. This has led to analysts dramatically increasing their estimates for data center power demand, as AI functionality becomes ubiquitous (Figure 5).

The expected surge in power demand has turned the 'sleepy' utility sector into a 'growth' sector, with some utility companies such as Constellation Energy, which signed an agreement to supply Microsoft (MSFT) with electricity by restarting the Three Mile Island reactor, massively outperforming the Magnificent 7 on a 3-year basis, as represented by the Roundhill Magnificent Seven ETF (MAGS) (Figure 6).

Evidently, the UTG fund has adapted its portfolio to take advantage of this new growth opportunity in utility stocks by adding exposure through CEG, Talen Energy (TLN), and Equinox, an operator of data centers. Kudos to UTG's management for their adept management of the fund.

Utilities Trade Like AI Stocks

However, with added AI growth exposure, utility stocks are also morphing into quasi-AI stocks. For example, year-to-date to April 8th (the market lows), the UTG fund and the Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLU) were trading in almost lockstep fashion with the MAGS ETF and the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) (Figure 7).

So, going forward, utility stocks may provide less of a market hedge than investors expect.

Valuations Are Getting Pricey

Moving on to valuations, the 2024 rally in utility stocks has boosted sector valuations to ~17-18x Fwd P/E (Figure 8). While it is not at the extreme levels reached in early 2022, it is starting to get pricey.

Arguably, with the improved growth prospects from increased AI-driven power demand, valuations may have room to rise further. The question is, is 32.9x Fwd GAAP P/E and 51.0x Fwd GAAP P/E too much to pay for CEG and TLN, respectively (Figure 9)?

However, with the S&P 500's valuation bouncing back to 21.4x Fwd P/E (Figure 10) following the Trump administration's tariff pauses, utility sector valuations are still relatively well-priced (Figure 11).

Conclusion

Back in early 2024, I was cautious about utility stocks due to rising regulatory risks, and downgraded the UTG fund to a hold recommendation. In hindsight, I did not anticipate the surge in investor interest in utility stocks as a play on AI-driven power demand.

As AI tools like ChatGPT become ubiquitous, analysts are dramatically raising their forecasts for power demand and have turned sleepy utility stocks into quasi-AI-growth stocks. While this has led to strong returns in the past year, valuations for select utility companies are reaching AI-bubble-like proportions, with CEG trading at a forward P/E of 33x.

Investors should also bear in mind that injecting AI-growth into the utility sector adds AI-volatility as well, with utility stocks trading in lockstep with the Magnificent 7 during the March-April market meltdown.

Looking forward, UTG's rising risks are balanced by attractive relative valuations. Therefore, I am maintaining my hold recommendation for now.

data.title: Cellnex Telecom Closing Valuation Gap With Peers Provides Upside Potential
data.description:
Introduction

Cellnex Telecom (OTCPK:CLNXF) (OTCPK:CLLNY) is the largest owner and manager of cell phone towers in Europe, with in excess of 100,000 towers in its asset base. As the company doesn’t have the same brand/name recognition as its peers American Tower (AMT) and Crown Castle (CCI), and as it doesn’t have the status of a REIT, it's trading at lower multiples.

I think that valuation gap could be closed in the next few years as Cellnex is kicking off a more shareholder friendly policy. Rather than continuously investing in growing the business, the company now also plans rather aggressive buybacks and has pledged to pay a minimum dividend of 500M EUR per year going forward. Although the initial dividend yield will be relatively low at 2%, I expect this will attract a new type of investor.

Cellnex has its primary listing in Spain, where it's trading with CLNX as its ticker symbol. The average daily volume in Spain is 1.4 million shares, representing a monetary value of 45M EUR. The CLLNY listing is the most liquid listing in North America with an average daily volume of 87,000 shares, representing almost $2M per day. Keep in mind - one CLLNY share represents half of the underlying share of Cellnex Telecom. The current market cap is approximately 22.6B EUR. I will use the Euro as base currency throughout this article.

A look at the Q1 results confirms the strong underlying cash flows

While the company does not publish a fully detailed financial report, it does provide shareholders with enough information on a quarterly basis to stay well-informed.

In the first quarter of this year, the company reported total revenue of 964M EUR, which is a 2% increase compared to the same quarter a year ago. Meanwhile, the operating expenses remained unchanged (they actually decreased by 0.5%), resulting in a 20M EUR increase in the adjusted EBITDA, and this represents an increase of just more than 2.5%.

As Cellnex is a lease-heavy company, it also makes sense to have a look at the EBITDA after lease payments (‘EBITDAaL’), which increased by in excess of 5% to 566M EUR.

And while the income statement shows a net loss of 49M EUR, which is higher than the net loss recorded in Q1 2024, that loss is not very relevant to the investment thesis as the non-recurring expenses increased by 90M EUR (the company would otherwise have been able to report a net profit). Additionally, it’s also very encouraging to see the net financial expenses decreased from 234M EUR to 196M EUR (although the cash interest expenses increased, as I will show later).

When an investor looks at Cellnex, it shouldn’t really focus on the net income. Although it looks like a REIT, Cellnex isn’t a REIT and is operating as a normal corporation. This means it needs to apply a depreciation schedule to the value of its cell phone towers, whereas its competitors/peers with a REIT status don’t have to do so.

That’s why it is more important to have a look at the recurring levered free cash flow result. This takes the maintenance capex into account, and that maintenance capex is of course substantially lower than the depreciation and amortization expenses.

As the image above shows, the starting point is the 798M EUR adjusted EBITDA, resulting in a 551M EUR recurring operating free cash flow of 551M EUR after taking the 15M EUR in maintenance capex into account. But what really matters is the recurring levered FCF as the interest expenses are obviously a large part of the cost basis and while the net cash interest expenses increased to 151M EUR.

All these elements combined resulted in an 8.6% decrease of the recurring levered FCF, but there's an important element here. As the image below shows, the first quarter is disproportionally heavy when it comes to cash lease payments and cash interest payments. In fact, the total interest payment in Q1 is higher than the payments in Q2 and Q3 combined.

This means we certainly cannot multiply the Q1 levered free cash flow result by 4 to calculate the anticipated full-year result. And that’s why the company’s guidance for this year comes in handy. As you can see below, Cellnex is guiding for a RLFCF of 1.9-1.95B EUR and a net free cash flow (after growth investments) of 280-380M EUR.

As the company’s asset base is in constant evolution, its updated pro forma guidance for 2025 (adjusted for changes in its Irish and Austrian asset base) calls for a RLFCF of 1.92-1.97B EUR, but the longer-term target to generate 2-2.2B EUR in RLFCF in 2027 remains unchanged.

The company’s new dividend policy may attract a new type of investor

Until the capital markets day of last year, the company was focusing on growth, and the vast majority of its free cash flow was spent on growth initiatives. While these growth investments still are a major cash drain, Cellnex is taking substantial steps to becoming more shareholder friendly.

It will pay another rather symbolic) dividend of 1.67 cents per share this year (as a final dividend for the FY 2024 results), but the company plans to buy back a substantial number of shares before embarking on a more generous dividend program. Cellnex plans to repurchase 800M EUR of its own shares. And assuming an average share price of 35 EUR, this would allow the company to repurchase and cancel about 23M shares, or just over 3% of its share count. That would reduce the net share count from the current 705M shares to around 685M shares (I’m also taking some dilution into account from stock-based compensation).

This buyback will mean two things. First of all, the Recurring Levered Free Cash flow per share will increase. Using 1.9B EUR for this year, using a 3% lower share count will increase this from 2.7 EUR per share to almost 2.8 EUR per share. That’s a good evolution.

Secondly, as the company is promising its shareholders a targeted minimum dividend of 500M EUR per year, a lower share count obviously also boosts the dividend per share. The minimum dividend payment has been established at 500M EUR per year and divided over 685M shares, this represents 73 cents per share for a yield of just over 2%.

While that dividend yield isn’t shocking, it’s important to keep in mind this is the "minimum" dividend and Cellnex may decide to make higher dividend payments. But given the anticipated strong cash flows (first on an underlying basis and subsequently also on a reported basis from 2027 on), it may make more sense for the company to continue to buy back stock as well.

Let’s assume Cellnex retires a net amount of 15M shares per year. That would result in a net share count of 655M shares by the end of 2027. Applying the midpoint of the anticipated RLFCF in 2027 of 2.1B EUR to a reduced share count would result in a RLFCF per share of 3.20 EUR. And this indicates the stock is trading at just over 10 times the recurring levered free cash flow (after taking sustaining capex into account).

We know its US counterparts (AMT and CCI) are currently trading at a multiple of 17.6 and 18.3 times their respective FFOs in FY 2027. Keep in mind the FFO result of both large tower companies does not include the maintenance capex, while the Cellnex RLFCF does take this into account. Applying the same multiple of approximately 18 times the FFO onto Cellnex’ RLFCF would indicate a fair value of 55 EUR based on a 685M share count and 57.6 EUR based on a 655M net share count by the end of 2027.

The risks associated with an investment in Cellnex Telecom

Cellnex has its tenants under long-term contracts, and those contracts include clauses that protect Cellnex against inflationary issues. As tenants like consistency, the churn tends to be very low as Cellnex is the largest provider of phone towers in Europe and the company enjoys an excellent reputation.

I think the main risk associated with an investment in Cellnex is an interest rate risk. As the company has approximately 18B EUR in gross financial debt (excluding lease liabilities), and a 100 bps increase in the average cost of debt would have a negative impact of almost 200M EUR per year, and close to 150M EUR per year on an after-tax basis. This could reduce the earnings by approximately 20–25 cents per share. Why this wouldn’t be a major headwind, it would definitely be a headwind.

Fortunately, most of the company’s debt was issued in Euro and the ECB has been reducing the benchmark interest rate for the past 12 months. The interest rates on the financial markets and bond markets are decreasing at a slower pace, but I don’t expect any major issues when the company has to refinance 430M EUR of debt this year and 1.7B EUR of debt in 2026.

Additionally, as the company’s RLFCF and reported FCF after growth initiatives continues to increase, the market

Investment thesis

Cellnex Telecom hasn’t paid a meaningful dividend in its history, but that’s changing now. While the dividend this year is rather symbolic (just a few cents per share, like the previous years), Cellnex has pledged to spend at least 500M EUR per year on cash dividends from next year on. This still doesn’t make the company a high-yield stock, but it may attract a different type of investor. Let’s also not forget the minimum dividend represents a payout ratio of just around 25% of the levered free cash flow result, so Cellnex has a lot of discretion to "throttle" the dividends (and share buybacks).

That’s why I have a long position in Cellnex and why I continue to write put options on market weakness. The stock is currently trading at just 12 times this year’s expected RLFCF, and this is expected to decrease to less than 11 based on the projections for 2027.

Its North American peers are trading at a substantially higher valuation and even if I would apply a 15% lower multiple and assign a multiple of 15 times the anticipated RLFCF, I end up with a fair value of 45-48 EUR by 2027. Using the midpoint of that target (46.5 EUR), the potential annualized return is approximately 18% per year for the next two years. Adding in the dividend would push the total return to in excess of 20% per year.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best International Stock Idea investment competition, which runs through June 6. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Chevron: Buy In Before The Rebound
data.description:
Chevron Corp. (NYSE:CVX) has not been able yet to rebound from the sharp valuation haircut the oil company suffered in April, leading to a 5% dividend yield that is now available to passive income investors.

Though crude oil prices also recently slumped and corrected down to $64/barrel, Chevron Corp. has high profitability and exposure to growth in a number of projects ranging from West Africa to the Gulf of America.

The oil company’s stock is selling for a mere 14.2 times 2026 estimated earnings, and Chevron Corp. is anticipated to grow its profits by 25% next year. The lack of an upside retracement in the last two months suggests unrealized upside potential for the stock of Chevron Corp. and the risk/reward relationship continues to be tilted upward, in my view.

In my last piece on Chevron Corp., I pointed to the upside that was inherent in the oil company’s upstream segment, which is the company expanded its production.

Chevron Corp.’s stock price has not recovered yet from April’s market convulsions, and I think this might be a good time to back up the truck with Chevron Corp.’s stock.

Production Growth, Upstream Profit Contraction

There are two conflicting factors at work within Chevron Corp.’s portfolio:

  1. Crude oil prices have continued to fall in 2025. While falling prices have weighed on upstream profitability, Chevron Corp. is compensating for a softer price backdrop by raising its production.
  2. In Q1'25, Chevron Corp. had a net production of 3,353 MboeD which the company is planning to grow 6-8% this year, primarily because of growth in the Permian, the Gulf of Mexico, and Kazakhstan.

The 5-quarter trend for Chevron Corp.’s upstream profits is negative, thanks to falling prices for crude oil. Presently, crude oil of West Texas Intermediate grade is selling for $63.60/barrel, but prices fell below the $60/barrel price market in April and May amid fears that new tariffs were dragging the world economy into a potential recession.

Crude oil prices have rebounded again lately, however, which could point to profit tailwinds for Chevron Corp. In the second quarter, if crude oil price increases prove to be of a lasting nature.

In the first quarter, Chevron Corp. produced $3.8 billion in profits from its upstream activities, which reflected a 28% decrease over the comparable period last year. Chevron Corp.’s Q1'25 downstream profits were $325 million and also decreased, by a total of 58%, thanks to lower margins on refined product sales.

That being said, Chevron Corp. maintained robust profitability and the company is starting up a couple of new projects that should yield production gains for the oil company moving forward. Among the new project startups are new operations in the Gulf of America (Whale and Ballymore), Australia (Wheatstone), and West Africa (Nigeria and Angola).

Though the trend in upstream profits is negative in the 5-quarter window shown below, Chevron Corp.’s profit prospects could brighten up in the event that U.S.-Sino relations improve and new trade uncertainty is avoided.

The U.S. economy is also profiting from gradually receding inflation (which decreased to just 2.3% in the month of April) and the U.S. economy is anticipated to grow in 2025, all of which could help a recovery in crude oil prices.

In addition, as mentioned, Chevron Corp. Is planning to see a high-single-digit increase in production in 2025, which could help the company counteract lower crude oil prices in its upstream segment.

Chevron Corp: Lack Of Upside Retracement Signifies Investment Opportunity

Shifting trade policy in the United States and the announcement of new tariffs on Liberation Day (April 2, 2025) have resulted in a substantial valuation correction for Chevron Corp as well as other oil companies in the U.S.

Chevron Corp.’s price has shown no signs of a recovery so far, but a bottom formation is clearly visible in the energy company’s stock chart, suggesting that the stock could be gathering strength for a breakout.

Chevron Corp. is selling for a 2026e profit multiple of 14.2 times and the oil company is expected to produce 25% profit growth next year, undoubtedly driven by the oil companies' multiple upstream projects around the world.

Chevron Corp.’s growth rate is the highest in the oil peer group as the market models only 20% growth for ExxonMobil Corp. (XOM) and half that, 10%, for ConocoPhillips (COP).

Though Chevron Corp. has the highest profit multiple, the stock offers passive income investors the most generous dividend yield of 5%, suggesting that the oil company might indeed be undervalued.

ExxonMobil Corp. is selling for 13.1 times, 2026 profit multiple, whereas ConocoPhillips changes hands for 12.1x times next year’s estimated earnings. Both companies have substantially lower yield offers for their shareholders.

In my view, Chevron Corp.’s high 5% yield could potentially establish a floor underneath the oil company’s present stock price and limit additional downside from here.

Moreover, Chevron Corp. is growing its dividend, which suggests that passive income investors a rising yield for their invested capital in the future.

Why The Investment Thesis Might Disappoint

A crude oil price correction to the downside could lead Chevron Corp. into a profit recession and put downward pressure on the oil company’s cash flow.

So far, Chevron Corp.’s earnings setup is still very decent as the company earned billions in profits in Q1'25, and with new project startups poised to add new production, Chevron Corp. might even see earnings and cash flow growth moving forward. That being said, periods of falling crude prices are poised to correlate with substantial valuation headwinds for the energy sector.

In the event of a drastic decrease in crude oil prices in 2025, Chevron Corp. is poised to see a severe multiple contraction.

My Takeaway

Chevron Corp. exhibits robust profitability in its upstream business, despite growing pressure on the company’s profits. Even with a decrease in crude oil prices, however, Chevron Corp. should be able to remain highly profitable and be able to afford a higher dividend for investors in 2025.

In my view, Chevron Corp. should be able to recover from the valuation haircut in the energy industry as trade uncertainty resolves and inflation continues to recede.

Though it is true that the energy company has seen falling upstream and downstream profits in the last year, catalyzed by lower crude oil prices, Chevron Corp. is still very much profitable and should be able to counteract at least some headwinds to earnings in a lower-price environment by growing its production.

Chevron Corp.’s lack of a valuation rebound is making the stock quite irresistible, as far as I am concerned, as the oil company is now paying investors a juicy 5% yield and investors potentially can ride a valuation rebound here as well. Buy.

data.title: QQA: New Option Strategy ETF In The Nasdaq 100
data.description:
QQA strategy

The Invesco QQQ Income Advantage ETF (NASDAQ:QQA) is an actively managed fund in the derivative income category launched on 07/17/2024. QQA has an attractive 12-month distribution yield of 8.63% and an expense ratio of 0.29% (waived from inception to 6/30/2025). Distributions are paid monthly.

As described by Invesco, the fund provides “exposure to the Nasdaq-100 Index combined with an active option income overlay for income generation”.

The fund invests in Nasdaq 100 stocks and up to 10% of its assets in equity-linked notes (ELNs) embedding options-based income strategies involving the Nasdaq 100 Index or ETFs tracking it. The fund may also hold a substantial part of assets in cash equivalents for risk management purposes.

ELNs are hybrid assets including equity securities and related options in a note (unsecured senior debt of the issuer). They typically embed covered calls and cash-secured puts strategies, meaning QQA delegates income generation to ELN issuers. It keeps the expense ratio low, although management costs of options strategies are passed in the performance of ELNs. Additionally, the fund may also directly invest in options and futures. This article will use as a benchmark the Nasdaq 100 Index, represented in the next chart by Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ).

Portfolio

As of writing, QQA has 76% of asset value in the Nasdaq 100 constituents with weights reflecting the index, about 19% in a money market fund, and about 5% in ELNs.

The top 10 companies, listed in the next table, represent 39.9% of assets and the three largest positions (Microsoft (MSFT), Nvidia (NVDA), and Apple (AAPL)) are between 5.7% and 6.6%.

Fundamentals

QQA is the smallest of these funds in assets under management. It has the cheapest expense ratio (on par with GPIQ), and the second-lowest yield. It comes in fourth position for total return and risk-adjusted return (measured by the Sharpe ratio in the table above) since July 2024. QQQI and QQQH have the best Sharpe ratios, on par, on this point. QQQI has a higher yield and return, while QQQH is more compelling for risk-averse investors, based on volatility and drawdown.

Takeaway

The Invesco QQQ Income Advantage ETF has an 8.63% yield from option strategies in the Nasdaq 100. QQA has 3/4 of assets in Nasdaq 100 stocks and delegates income generation to equity-linked notes. QQA is almost on par with QQQ in total return since July 2024. However, in this short track record, QQQI and QQQH look more compelling than QQA, the former for its higher yield and return, the latter for its lower volatility and shallower drawdown.

data.title: The Jobs Report Did Not Indicate Strength
data.description:
Stocks surged on Friday with news that the US economy created 139,000 jobs in May, which was modestly higher than the consensus estimate of 126,000. The unemployment rate held steady at 4.2%, while wages increased 0.4% for the month and 3.9% over the past year. That is an uptick from the 3.8% rate the month before. Evidently, this report shelved concerns about a downturn in economic activity, due to tariffs and the ongoing trade war. As a result, investors drove the S&P 500 index above 6,000 for the first time since February, and bond yields soared with the 10-year Treasury yield back above 4.5%, while the 30-year yield neared 5%.

If Friday’s stock market surge was based on a strong jobs report for May, it was a false premise. The jobs report did not indicate strength. Even if it did, employment is a lagging indicator that tells us very little about what lies ahead. Investors would be better served to focus on leading indicators but first let me explain why this report was not as strong as reported.

The Bureau of Labor Statistics estimates how many jobs are created each month with a margin of error that ranges +/- 100,000 jobs. As more data is collected, the number is revised. The direction of the revisions gives you a sense of the underlying trend. Little mention was made of the fact that the number of jobs created in the prior two months were revised down by 95,000. That means the net job gain for May was 44,000. This is more in line with the 37,000 private jobs that the ADP payroll report indicates were created. It is estimated that the economy needs to create at least 100,000 jobs per month to absorb new entrants into the workforce. Based on recent data, we continue to accomplish that goal, but more timely data raises doubts.

While weekly unemployment claims are still within the range they have held the past two years, claims have been in an uptrend since the beginning of the year. We need to exceed 260,000 to raise serious concerns.

The uptrend in claims is likely to continue based on job cut announcements reported by Challenger, Grey & Christmas, which have risen significantly this year. In May, there were 22,492 job cuts announced in the service sector, which is the highest total for one month in May 2020. The service sector is our engine of growth. Furthermore, employers have cut 696,309 jobs so far this year, which is an 80% increase from the year-to-date number one year ago. This is a significantly negative rate of change. According to Andrew Challenger, “tariffs, funding cuts, consumer spending, and overall economic pessimism are putting intense pressure on companies’ workforces.”

This doesn’t signify economic strength, nor does the business optimism survey from the Association of International Certified Professional Accountants. In May, only 27% of executives were confident about the economic outlook over the coming year, which is down from 47% during the first quarter and 67% in the fourth quarter of last year. This is leading to delayed hiring and expansion plans.

Therefore, if we want to try to explain why stock prices surged on Friday, I think the jobs report for May from the BLS is a very poor excuse. Instead, I think it is nothing more than pure momentum chasing momentum, which runs counter to deteriorating fundamentals. That said, the labor market is still holding up, and wage growth of 3.9% indicated that workers are still realizing real (inflation-adjusted) wage growth. This should help to sustain the economic expansion. That said, the jobs report is not a lagging indicator.

Lost on most economists is the fact that consumer spending is the primary driver of job creation and not the other way around. Granted, more jobs lead to more income, which then leads to more consumer spending, but new jobs are a function if increased demand for goods and services. Those jobs are not created out of thin air. Therefore, investors should be focusing on consumer spending growth, which is the tip of the spear in terms of leading economic indicators.

Spending has been resilient through April, but the strength was likely bolstered by consumers who were trying to front-run price increases from tariffs in the same way that businesses ramped imports to build pre-tariff inventories. That is likely to result in a downturn in spending growth this summer, which is aggravated by price increases from tariffs. Then we will see an increase in job losses and unemployment claims. Finally, the BLS jobs numbers will reflect these developments at summer’s end or in the fall.

data.title: GameStop, Oracle Headline Earnings Next Week As Investors Eye Trade Talks, Inflation Data, WWDC25
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Wall Street's focus next week will be on further trade developments and key inflation data. U.S. and Chinese officials including U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, Secretary of Commerce Howard Lutnick and Trade Representative Ambassador Jamieson Greer are set to meet in London on Monday. On Wednesday, the consumer price index report for May will be published, followed by the producer price index report on Thursday. Also grabbing the spotlight will be Apple's (AAPL) WWDC 2025 event, kicking off on Monday. The event is expected to feature major announcements and a first look at new updates for iOS, iPadOS, macOS, watchOS, tvOS, and visionOS.

Video game retailer GameStop (GME), cloud software firm Oracle (ORCL) and Photoshop creator Adobe (ADBE) will report earnings next week.

______________________________________________________________________

Earnings spotlight: Monday, June 9: Casey's General Stores (CASY), Calavo Growers (CVGW), Lakeland Industries (LAKE). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday, June 10: GameStop, J.M. Smucker (SJM), United Natural Foods (UNFI), GitLab (GTLB). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday, June 11: Oracle, Chewy (CHWY), SailPoint (SAIL). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday, June 12: Adobe, Lovesac (LOVE). See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: Applied Digital (APLD) and Ondas Holdings (ONDS) have a higher level of implied volatility than normal based on options trading. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include SafeSpace Global (OTCPK:SSGC), M3-Brigade Acquisition V. (MBAV), and Regulus Therapeutics (RGLS). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Brown-Forman Corporation (BF.A), Abacus Global Management (ABL) and Arrive AI (ARAI).

Dividend watch: Companies that have an ex-dividend next week include Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL), Steven Madden (SHOO), Ross Stores (ROST), Nvidia (NVDA), Domino's Pizza (DPZ), and Gilead Sciences (GILD). Read through some dividend stock picks from Seeking Alpha analysts.

IPO watch: San Francisco-based fintech company Chime Financial (CHYM) is expected to price its IPO and begin trading. At the top end of the expected range, Chime could raise up to $832 million, with a fully diluted valuation of about $11 billion. Voyager Technologies (VOYG) is also set to launch its IPO. The Denver-based aerospace and defense company is targeting a valuation of approximately $1.6 billion. The quiet period ends on eToro Group (ETOR) to free up analysts to post ratings. The IPO lock-up period expires for certain blocks of shares of ServiceTitan (TTAN).

Investor Events: Apple's WWDC 2025 event in Cupertino, California will take the spotlight next week, scheduled from June 9 to 13. The event is expected to feature major announcements and a first look at new updates for iOS, iPadOS, macOS, watchOS, tvOS, and visionOS. The Databricks Data + AI Summit will run from June 9 to 12, featuring talks from top executives from Databricks, JPMorgan Chase (JPM) CEO Jamie Dimon, Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei, and Microsoft (MSFT) CEO Satya Nadella.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
